articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Constitutionality of Delhi Services Act, Reinforcing Parliament's Authority
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark verdict, has upheld the constitutional validity of the Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi (Amendment) Act, 2023.
• The ruling reaffirms the Parliament's legislative competence over services in the NCT of Delhi, settling the long-standing dispute between the Union Government and the Delhi elected government.
• The court emphasized the unique status of Delhi as a Union Territory with a legislative assembly, balancing federal principles with the special administrative needs of the national capital.
• This decision is expected to streamline governance in Delhi, providing clarity on the administrative control over bureaucrats and their postings.
• Critics argue the judgment might further curtail the powers of the elected government, while proponents welcome the clarity it brings to Delhi's administrative structure.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary—Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
keyTerms: Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi Act (GNCTD Act)
prelimsPoints: The GNCTD (Amendment) Act, 2023 was challenged in the Supreme Court.
The Act pertains to the control over services (bureaucracy) in the NCT of Delhi.
Article 239AA of the Constitution grants special status to Delhi.
The ruling clarifies the legislative competence of Parliament over Delhi's services.
A Constitutional Bench delivered the judgment.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on the balance of power between the elected government of Delhi and the Union government.
Discuss the challenges of administering a Union Territory with a legislative assembly, citing the Delhi model.
Evaluate the federal principles at play in the context of Article 239AA and the unique status of Delhi.
Examine how such judgments impact cooperative federalism and the autonomy of state/UT governments.
articles.imageDescription: Gavel and Indian Constitution in the backdrop of the Supreme Court building.
articles.trivia: Delhi became a Union Territory in 1956 and was given a legislative assembly in 1991 through the 69th Constitutional Amendment Act, which inserted Article 239AA.

articles.headline: India's Q2 FY26 GDP Growth Projected at 6.8%, Driven by Manufacturing and Services
articles.explainer:
• Leading economic indicators suggest India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew by an estimated 6.8% in the second quarter (July-September) of Financial Year 2025-26.
• The manufacturing sector witnessed robust expansion, bolstered by continued government incentives and strong domestic demand.
• The services sector, particularly IT, financial services, and tourism, maintained its growth momentum, contributing significantly to the overall economic performance.
• Agricultural output is expected to show steady growth, supported by a favorable monsoon, though global commodity price volatility remains a concern.
• While inflation remains a monitored factor, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is cautiously optimistic about achieving its target range amidst global economic uncertainties.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: Q2 FY26 covers July-September 2025.
GDP growth rate projected at 6.8%.
Key drivers: Manufacturing, Services.
RBI's role in managing inflation and growth.
Monsoon impact on agricultural output.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's economic resilience in the face of global headwinds.
Discuss the role of government policies (e.g., PLI schemes) in boosting the manufacturing sector.
Examine the challenges that India's economy might face in achieving sustained high growth, including inflation and global supply chain disruptions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of RBI's monetary policy stance in balancing growth and price stability.
articles.imageDescription: Graph depicting India's GDP growth trend with manufacturing and services sector indicators.
articles.trivia: India is projected to become the world's third-largest economy by 2027.

articles.headline: India and Brazil Announce Joint Satellite Mission for Climate Monitoring and Disaster Management
articles.explainer:
• India (ISRO) and Brazil (AEB) have formalized an agreement for a joint satellite mission aimed at enhancing climate monitoring and disaster management capabilities.
• The mission will involve the development and launch of an advanced earth observation satellite, integrating ISRO's expertise in satellite technology with Brazil's extensive experience in Amazon rainforest and agricultural monitoring.
• This collaboration underscores the growing strategic partnership between the two BRICS nations in critical areas like space technology and environmental sustainability.
• Data from the satellite will be crucial for tracking deforestation, agricultural productivity, water resource management, and providing early warnings for natural disasters.
• The initiative is also a step towards strengthening South-South cooperation in space science and its applications for global challenges.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: ISRO (India) and AEB (Brazil) are collaborating.
The mission is for climate monitoring and disaster management.
It involves an advanced earth observation satellite.
Brazil is a BRICS member.
Applications include deforestation tracking and early warning systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's growing space diplomacy and its role in fostering South-South cooperation.
Analyze how joint space missions contribute to addressing global challenges like climate change and disaster resilience.
Examine the strategic implications of India-Brazil partnership in advanced technologies for both nations.
Evaluate the potential economic and environmental benefits of such collaborations for developing countries.
articles.imageDescription: Conceptual image of a satellite orbiting Earth with national flags of India and Brazil.
articles.trivia: Brazil was the first South American nation to launch its own satellite, SCD-1, in 1993. India has launched satellites for several international partners.

articles.headline: Government Unveils Phase II of Project Tiger Revival Plan, Focus on Corridor Connectivity and Local Communities
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has launched the second phase of its ambitious Project Tiger revival plan, aiming for enhanced conservation efforts.
• This phase places a strong emphasis on securing vital tiger corridors to facilitate genetic exchange and prevent habitat fragmentation, crucial for long-term population viability.
• A key component involves greater engagement with local communities, including improved livelihood opportunities and participatory conservation models, to reduce human-wildlife conflict.
• Advanced technologies like AI-powered surveillance, drone monitoring, and predictive analytics will be integrated for more effective anti-poaching measures and habitat management.
• The plan also targets the restoration of degraded forest areas within and around tiger reserves to augment prey base and overall ecosystem health.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Project Tiger was launched in 1973.
Phase II focuses on corridor connectivity and community involvement.
Advanced technologies like AI and drones are part of the plan.
Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change is the nodal agency.
Aim is to reduce habitat fragmentation and human-wildlife conflict.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution of tiger conservation strategies in India, highlighting the shift towards community-centric and technology-driven approaches.
Analyze the ecological and socio-economic challenges in maintaining tiger corridors and mitigating human-wildlife conflict.
Evaluate the role of local communities and indigenous knowledge in successful wildlife conservation programs.
Examine how technological advancements can be effectively deployed for biodiversity protection and anti-poaching efforts.
articles.imageDescription: A majestic tiger walking through a dense forest, symbolizing conservation efforts.
articles.trivia: India accounts for over 70% of the world's wild tiger population. The 'M-STrIPES' system is used for monitoring tigers in reserves.

articles.headline: Global Digital Public Infrastructure Summit Kicks Off in Delhi, India Showcases UPI and Aadhaar Model
articles.explainer:
• New Delhi is hosting the inaugural Global Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) Summit, bringing together policymakers, tech leaders, and international organizations.
• India is prominently showcasing its successful DPI models, including the Unified Payments Interface (UPI), Aadhaar, and DigiLocker, as examples for developing nations.
• The summit aims to foster international cooperation in building inclusive, secure, and interoperable DPIs to accelerate digital transformation and achieve Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
• Discussions are centered on governance frameworks, data privacy, cyber security challenges, and equitable access to digital services.
• Several developing countries have expressed keen interest in adopting elements of the 'India Stack' to build their own digital ecosystems.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Information Technology, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
prelimsPoints: The summit is being held in New Delhi.
India is presenting its DPI models: UPI, Aadhaar, DigiLocker.
DPI aims for inclusive digital transformation and SDGs.
Key topics include data privacy and cybersecurity.
The 'India Stack' is a reference architecture for DPI.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in achieving inclusive growth and sustainable development.
Analyze how India's 'India Stack' has revolutionized financial inclusion and public service delivery.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for developing countries in adopting and implementing DPIs, focusing on data privacy and cybersecurity concerns.
Evaluate India's role as a leader in digital public goods and its potential to foster global digital cooperation.
articles.imageDescription: A world map with digital connections, highlighting India's digital innovation.
articles.trivia: UPI processed over 10 billion transactions in a single month in 2023, making it one of the world's most successful real-time payment systems.

articles.headline: Government Reviews Progress on 500 GW Renewable Energy Target by 2030, Identifies Bottlenecks
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy held a high-level review meeting to assess the progress towards India's ambitious target of 500 GW renewable energy capacity by 2030.
• While significant strides have been made in solar and wind power installations, challenges related to land acquisition, grid integration, and financing were highlighted.
• The review emphasized the need for accelerated deployment of battery energy storage systems (BESS) and pumped hydro projects to ensure grid stability with higher renewable penetration.
• New policy measures are being considered to streamline regulatory approvals and attract further private investment into the renewable energy sector.
• India's commitment to climate change mitigation and energy security heavily relies on achieving this target, moving away from fossil fuels.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: India's target is 500 GW renewable energy by 2030.
Ministry of New and Renewable Energy is the nodal ministry.
Challenges include land acquisition and grid integration.
Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) are crucial.
The target is part of India's climate commitments.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's 500 GW renewable energy target in its climate action strategy and energy security.
Analyze the major bottlenecks hindering the accelerated deployment of renewable energy capacity in India.
Examine the role of energy storage solutions in ensuring grid stability with a high penetration of intermittent renewable energy sources.
Suggest policy recommendations to overcome financing and regulatory challenges in the renewable energy sector.
articles.imageDescription: Solar panels at a large-scale solar farm, symbolizing renewable energy.
articles.trivia: India's installed renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro) crossed 125 GW by 2023.

articles.headline: India-Japan 2+2 Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Maritime Security and Defence Technology Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue involving foreign and defence ministers concluded in Tokyo, focusing on strengthening strategic ties.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, expressing concerns over regional security challenges.
• Key agreements were reached on enhancing maritime security cooperation, including joint exercises and intelligence sharing in the Indian Ocean and Pacific.
• Discussions also covered collaboration in defence technology, co-production, and joint research and development, particularly in advanced materials and AI for defence applications.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between India and Japan in maintaining regional stability and economic prosperity.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: The 2+2 Dialogue involves Foreign and Defence Ministers.
Held in Tokyo.
Focus on Indo-Pacific, maritime security.
Cooperation in defence technology and joint R&D discussed.
India and Japan are members of the Quad.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-Japan 2+2 dialogue in strengthening their 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
Discuss how India and Japan's collaboration contributes to the vision of a free and open Indo-Pacific.
Examine the potential for defence technology cooperation between India and Japan, considering India's 'Make in India' initiative.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in bilateral security cooperation amidst evolving geopolitical dynamics.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan side by side, symbolizing diplomatic relations.
articles.trivia: The first India-Japan 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue was held in 2019.

articles.headline: National Urban Flood Resilience Mission Launched to Combat Growing Climate Risks
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government has launched the National Urban Flood Resilience Mission, a multi-pronged initiative to address the increasing threat of urban flooding across Indian cities.
• The mission focuses on integrated urban planning, upgrading stormwater drainage systems, and promoting permeable infrastructure to enhance water absorption capacity.
• It will leverage advanced meteorological forecasting models and real-time data analytics for early warning systems and efficient disaster response.
• Capacity building for municipal bodies and community preparedness programs are integral components, aiming to create 'flood-smart' cities.
• The initiative acknowledges the severe economic and social disruption caused by recent urban floods, linking it directly to climate change impacts and unplanned urbanization.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: The mission targets urban flooding.
Focus on integrated urban planning and drainage upgrades.
Uses advanced forecasting and real-time data.
Aims for 'flood-smart' cities.
Addresses climate change impacts and unplanned urbanization.
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to the increasing frequency and intensity of urban floods in India.
Analyze the key components and expected outcomes of the National Urban Flood Resilience Mission.
Evaluate the role of good governance, community participation, and technological integration in effective urban disaster management.
Suggest sustainable urban planning strategies that can enhance the resilience of Indian cities against climate-induced disasters.
articles.imageDescription: City street partially submerged in water, showing the impact of urban flooding.
articles.trivia: Chennai, Mumbai, and Bengaluru are among the Indian cities most vulnerable to urban flooding.

articles.headline: Ministry of Defence Releases New Positive Indigenisation List, Boosts Domestic Manufacturing
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence has unveiled its fifth 'Positive Indigenisation List', comprising over 100 more military systems and components that will be procured only from domestic sources.
• This move aims to further reduce India's reliance on foreign defence imports, bolster the 'Make in India' initiative, and strengthen the indigenous defence industrial base.
• The list includes critical technologies and platforms, with staggered timelines for their indigenization, encouraging private sector participation and innovation.
• The policy is expected to create significant employment opportunities, foster technological self-reliance, and enhance India's strategic autonomy.
• Industry experts welcome the clarity and long-term vision provided by these lists, urging for consistent policy support and timely procurement cycles.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: Fifth Positive Indigenisation List released by Ministry of Defence.
Includes over 100 military systems/components.
Aims to reduce foreign defence imports.
Supports 'Make in India' in defence.
Encourages private sector participation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic rationale behind India's push for defence indigenization and its implications for national security.
Analyze the challenges faced by the Indian defence industry in achieving self-reliance and suggest measures to overcome them.
Evaluate the role of Positive Indigenisation Lists in fostering a robust domestic defence manufacturing ecosystem.
Examine how defence indigenization can contribute to economic growth and job creation in India.
articles.imageDescription: Modern fighter jet designed and manufactured in India.
articles.trivia: India aims to achieve a defence manufacturing turnover of $25 billion by 2025, including $5 billion in exports.

articles.headline: Progress on India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor Discussed at Bilateral Meeting
articles.explainer:
• High-level officials from India and key Gulf nations held discussions to review the progress and expedite implementation of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC).
• The meeting focused on finalizing investment frameworks, logistical agreements, and regulatory harmonization to ensure seamless connectivity across the corridor.
• IMEC is envisioned as a transformative infrastructure project, aiming to enhance trade, energy flow, and digital connectivity between Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.
• Challenges related to geopolitical stability, funding mechanisms, and coordination among multiple stakeholders were addressed.
• The corridor is seen as a strategic response to global supply chain vulnerabilities and a significant step towards greater economic integration.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: IMEC connects India, Middle East, and Europe.
Aims to enhance trade, energy, and digital connectivity.
Involves multiple nations and stakeholders.
Discussions focused on investment and logistics.
Seen as a response to supply chain vulnerabilities.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) for India.
Discuss the potential geopolitical implications of IMEC in the context of global power dynamics and competing connectivity initiatives.
Examine the challenges in implementing a large-scale multimodal infrastructure project like IMEC involving multiple countries and diverse interests.
Evaluate how IMEC could impact global trade routes, energy security, and regional economic development.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the proposed route of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor.
articles.trivia: IMEC was announced during the G20 Summit in New Delhi in September 2023.

articles.headline: National Mission on Advanced Materials Launched to Boost Indigenous Research and Manufacturing
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Science & Technology (DST) has launched the 'National Mission on Advanced Materials' to catalyze indigenous research, development, and manufacturing in critical material science.
• The mission aims to bridge the gap between fundamental research and industrial application, focusing on areas like advanced ceramics, composites, smart materials, and materials for clean energy.
• It will foster collaboration between academic institutions, national laboratories, and industries, creating a robust ecosystem for material innovation.
• Funding mechanisms, skill development programs, and intellectual property protection are key pillars of the mission, designed to make India a leader in advanced materials.
• These materials are crucial for strategic sectors such as defence, aerospace, electronics, and renewable energy, impacting national security and economic competitiveness.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Launched by Department of Science & Technology (DST).
Focus on indigenous R&D and manufacturing of advanced materials.
Covers ceramics, composites, smart materials, clean energy materials.
Aims to foster academia-industry collaboration.
Crucial for defence, aerospace, electronics, renewable energy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of advanced materials for India's economic growth and national security.
Analyze how the National Mission on Advanced Materials can enhance India's self-reliance in critical technologies.
Examine the challenges in translating material science research into industrial applications in India.
Suggest policy interventions to accelerate innovation and commercialization in the advanced materials sector.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract image representing molecular structures and advanced materials.
articles.trivia: Carbon nanotubes, graphene, and high-temperature superconductors are examples of advanced materials with revolutionary potential.

articles.headline: GST Council Convenes, Discusses Rate Rationalization and Compliance Simplification
articles.explainer:
• The Goods and Services Tax (GST) Council held its latest meeting, addressing key issues related to rate rationalization and simplifying compliance procedures for businesses.
• Deliberations included potential adjustments to GST slabs to reduce the number of rates and make the tax structure more efficient and less complex.
• Measures to enhance technology-driven compliance, curb evasion, and facilitate input tax credit mechanisms were also on the agenda.
• States raised concerns regarding revenue implications of rate changes and sought assurances on compensation mechanisms for any potential losses.
• The Council aims to balance revenue augmentation for the government with ease of doing business for taxpayers, especially MSMEs.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
prelimsPoints: GST Council is a constitutional body (Article 279A).
Headed by Union Finance Minister.
Discussed rate rationalization and compliance simplification.
States' revenue concerns were addressed.
Aims to benefit MSMEs.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and challenges of GST rate rationalization in India.
Analyze the impact of GST on different sectors of the Indian economy, especially MSMEs.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the GST Council as a federal institution for indirect tax governance.
Suggest measures to further simplify GST compliance and curb tax evasion in India.
articles.imageDescription: Indian rupee currency notes with GST logo, symbolizing tax reforms.
articles.trivia: GST was implemented in India on July 1, 2017, replacing multiple indirect taxes.

articles.headline: Government Proposes Amendments to Lokpal Act to Strengthen Anti-Corruption Framework
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government has initiated consultations for proposed amendments to the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013, aiming to enhance its operational effectiveness and jurisdiction.
• The amendments seek to address identified lacunae in the existing law, particularly concerning the appointment process, investigative powers, and timely disposal of corruption cases.
• Discussions include broadening the scope of individuals covered under the Lokpal's ambit and ensuring greater autonomy and financial independence for the institution.
• The move comes amidst ongoing efforts to bolster India's anti-corruption framework and promote transparency and accountability in public administration.
• Critics emphasize the need for robust safeguards to prevent misuse of power and ensure due process during investigations.
gsSubTopics: Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Transparency & Accountability and institutional and other measures.
prelimsPoints: Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 established the Lokpal.
Amendments aim to enhance operational effectiveness and jurisdiction.
Focus on appointment process, investigative powers, and case disposal.
Seeks to broaden Lokpal's ambit.
Part of India's anti-corruption efforts.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the Lokpal institution in promoting good governance and combating corruption in India.
Analyze the challenges faced by the Lokpal since its inception and how the proposed amendments seek to address them.
Evaluate the need for a strong, independent anti-corruption ombudsman in a democratic setup.
Examine the balance between empowering anti-corruption bodies and ensuring safeguards against potential misuse of power.
articles.imageDescription: Logo of Lokpal of India, symbolizing anti-corruption efforts.
articles.trivia: The concept of an ombudsman originated in Sweden in the early 19th century.

articles.headline: Review of National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Highlights Progress and Areas for Improvement
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a comprehensive review of the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, three years after its launch.
• Significant progress has been noted in curriculum reforms, integration of vocational education, and the use of technology for learning and administration.
• Challenges persist in achieving universal foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN), faculty recruitment, and infrastructure development, especially in rural areas.
• The review emphasized the need for greater state-level ownership and resource allocation to effectively translate policy vision into ground-level impact.
• NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet the needs of the 21st century, focusing on holistic development and critical thinking.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 was launched in 2020.
Review conducted by Ministry of Education.
Progress in curriculum and vocational education.
Challenges in FLN, faculty, infrastructure.
Emphasizes state ownership and resource allocation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in shaping India's future human capital.
Analyze the key achievements and persistent challenges in the initial years of NEP 2020 implementation.
Examine the role of technology and vocational education in making India's education system more inclusive and employment-oriented.
Suggest strategies for effective state-level implementation and resource mobilization for NEP 2020 objectives.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom, symbolizing education reform.
articles.trivia: The NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education, which was modified in 1992.

articles.headline: Permanent Indus Commission Meeting Underway to Address Water-Sharing Issues
articles.explainer:
• The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC), comprising water commissioners from India and Pakistan, has commenced its annual meeting to discuss various aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT).
• The agenda includes technical discussions on flood data sharing, usage of waters of the western rivers, and resolution of minor disputes related to hydroelectric projects.
• The meeting aims to ensure continued cooperation and adherence to the provisions of the IWT, a crucial water-sharing agreement between the two nations.
• While the treaty has largely withstood geopolitical tensions, concerns over project designs and hydrological data exchange often surface during these discussions.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank, is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties globally, despite intermittent bilateral strains.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) meets annually.
Discusses Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) between India and Pakistan.
IWT was brokered by the World Bank.
Eastern rivers for India, Western rivers for Pakistan (with some Indian usage rights).
Agenda includes flood data and hydroelectric projects.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance and enduring success of the Indus Waters Treaty amidst India-Pakistan geopolitical tensions.
Analyze the role of the Permanent Indus Commission in resolving water-sharing disputes and ensuring treaty compliance.
Examine the challenges and potential areas of conflict related to the IWT in the context of climate change and increasing water demand.
Evaluate the importance of water diplomacy in maintaining regional stability in South Asia.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River System and the distribution of rivers under the IWT.
articles.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty was signed in Karachi on September 19, 1960, by Indian Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru and Pakistani President Ayub Khan.

articles.headline: ASI Report Highlights Growing Threat of Climate Change to India's Ancient Heritage Sites
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) has released a comprehensive report detailing the escalating threats posed by climate change to India's vast array of ancient monuments and heritage sites.
• Extreme weather events, including increased rainfall, floods, droughts, and rising sea levels, are accelerating erosion, structural damage, and the degradation of historical structures.
• The report identifies coastal sites, ancient caves, and monuments built with sensitive materials as particularly vulnerable, requiring urgent adaptive conservation strategies.
• Recommendations include the use of advanced climate modeling, sustainable construction practices for restoration, and the establishment of buffer zones around vulnerable sites.
• This highlights the critical intersection of cultural heritage preservation and environmental sustainability, urging for integrated policy approaches.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Report by Archaeological Survey of India (ASI).
Highlights climate change threat to heritage sites.
Threats include erosion, structural damage from extreme weather.
Coastal sites and caves are highly vulnerable.
Recommends climate modeling and sustainable restoration.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted impacts of climate change on India's tangible cultural heritage and its implications for conservation efforts.
Analyze the challenges faced by organizations like ASI in protecting ancient monuments from climate-induced degradation.
Examine the need for integrated policy frameworks that combine cultural heritage preservation with environmental sustainability.
Suggest innovative and sustainable strategies for the adaptive conservation of vulnerable heritage sites in India.
articles.imageDescription: Ancient temple ruins showing signs of weathering and erosion.
articles.trivia: The Ajanta Caves, Elephanta Caves, and the Sun Temple at Konark are among the UNESCO World Heritage Sites managed by the ASI.

articles.headline: India-Sri Lanka Fisheries Dialogue Resumes to Resolve Long-Standing Disputes
articles.explainer:
• A fresh round of bilateral talks between India and Sri Lanka has commenced to address the contentious issue of fishing rights and persistent disputes in the Palk Strait.
• The dialogue aims to find a permanent solution to the problem of Indian fishermen allegedly poaching in Sri Lankan waters, leading to arrests and seizure of boats.
• Discussions include exploring alternative livelihood options for Indian fishermen, joint patrolling mechanisms, and sustainable fishing practices.
• Both sides acknowledge the humanitarian aspect of the issue while emphasizing the need to protect marine resources and the livelihoods of their respective fishing communities.
• Resolving this issue is crucial for maintaining cordial bilateral relations and regional maritime stability.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Talks between India and Sri Lanka on fishing rights.
Disputes occur in the Palk Strait.
Issue of Indian fishermen allegedly poaching in Sri Lankan waters.
Katchatheevu Island is a relevant geographical point.
Focus on sustainable fishing and alternative livelihoods.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and complexities of the India-Sri Lanka fisheries dispute in the Palk Strait.
Analyze the socio-economic implications of the dispute for fishing communities on both sides.
Evaluate the effectiveness of various diplomatic and policy measures taken to resolve this long-standing issue.
Suggest sustainable solutions that can balance the livelihoods of fishermen with marine ecological conservation.
articles.imageDescription: Fishing boats in the Palk Strait, symbolizing the disputed waters.
articles.trivia: Katchatheevu, an uninhabited island in the Palk Strait, was ceded to Sri Lanka by India in 1974.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Telemedicine Services to Remote Rural Areas
articles.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) has announced a significant expansion of its telemedicine services, specifically targeting remote and underserved rural areas.
• This initiative aims to bridge the healthcare access gap by leveraging digital platforms to connect patients with doctors and specialists across the country.
• The expansion includes setting up more digital health kiosks, training local health workers in digital literacy, and ensuring last-mile internet connectivity.
• It seeks to reduce the burden on tertiary care hospitals and provide timely medical consultations, especially for non-critical conditions and follow-ups.
• The move aligns with the broader goal of universal health coverage and equitable access to quality healthcare services.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: ABDM is expanding telemedicine services.
Targets remote rural areas.
Aims to bridge healthcare access gap.
Includes digital health kiosks and internet connectivity.
Contributes to universal health coverage.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of telemedicine in transforming healthcare delivery in rural India.
Analyze the role of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) in achieving equitable and accessible healthcare.
Examine the challenges in implementing digital health solutions in remote areas, including digital literacy and infrastructure.
Suggest measures to ensure the effective and inclusive adoption of telemedicine services across India.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor conducting a telemedicine consultation with a patient in a rural setting via a tablet.
articles.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission aims to create a seamless online platform for the entire healthcare ecosystem.

articles.headline: Exhibition on Ancient Indian Metallurgy Showcases Advanced Techniques and Innovations
articles.explainer:
• A new national exhibition, 'Dhaatu: The Legacy of Indian Metallurgy', has opened, showcasing the remarkable achievements and advanced techniques of ancient Indian metallurgy.
• The exhibition features artifacts from the Harappan civilization, Gupta period, and medieval India, highlighting sophisticated methods of metal extraction, alloying, and crafting.
• Displays include intricate bronze sculptures, Wootz steel artifacts, and examples of rust-resistant iron, demonstrating India's pioneering role in metallurgical science.
• Experts emphasize the need to revive and integrate traditional knowledge systems with modern scientific research for sustainable material development.
• This cultural initiative aims to educate the public about India's rich scientific heritage and inspire future generations in science and technology.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.

Showcases ancient Indian metallurgical techniques.
Artifacts from Harappan, Gupta, and medieval periods.
Highlights Wootz steel and rust-resistant iron.
Aims to promote India's scientific heritage.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significant contributions of ancient India to the field of metallurgy and their global impact.
Analyze how traditional Indian metallurgical knowledge can be integrated with modern scientific research for sustainable development.
Examine the importance of cultural exhibitions in promoting awareness and appreciation of India's scientific and technological heritage.
Evaluate the factors that led to the advanced state of metallurgy in ancient India.
articles.imageDescription: Ancient Indian bronze sculpture with intricate details.
articles.trivia: The Iron Pillar of Delhi, erected in the 4th century CE, is famous for its high resistance to corrosion, a testament to ancient Indian metallurgical skill.

highlights: Supreme Court upholds Delhi Services Act, reinforcing Parliament's authority over NCT administration.
India's Q2 FY26 GDP growth projected at 6.8%, driven by robust manufacturing and services sectors.
India and Brazil announce joint satellite mission for enhanced climate monitoring and disaster management.
Phase II of Project Tiger revival plan launched, focusing on corridor connectivity and community engagement.
Global Digital Public Infrastructure Summit in Delhi showcases India's UPI and Aadhaar models for global adoption.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent Supreme Court verdict on the Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi (Amendment) Act, 2023, consider the following statements:
1. The verdict explicitly states that the Delhi elected government has full control over 'services' in the NCT of Delhi.
2. The judgment reaffirms the Parliament's legislative competence over services in the NCT of Delhi.
3. Article 239AA of the Indian Constitution grants special status to Delhi.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The Supreme Court upheld the constitutional validity of the Act, which grants the Union government (via the Lieutenant Governor) control over services, thus reaffirming Parliament's authority, not the Delhi elected government's full control. Statement 2 is correct as the verdict reinforces Parliament's legislative competence. Statement 3 is correct, as Article 239AA specifically deals with the special provisions for Delhi.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about India's renewable energy targets and challenges is/are correct?
1. India aims to achieve 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.
2. The primary challenge in achieving this target is insufficient financial investment from the private sector.
3. Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) are crucial for ensuring grid stability with high renewable energy penetration.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; India has a target of 500 GW renewable energy capacity by 2030. Statement 2 is incorrect; while private investment is a factor, the primary challenges identified often include land acquisition, grid integration, and regulatory hurdles, not solely insufficient private investment. Statement 3 is correct; BESS and pumped hydro projects are vital for managing the intermittency of renewable sources and maintaining grid stability.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the 'India Stack':
1. It is a set of open APIs and digital public goods that aims for digital transformation.
2. Aadhaar and Unified Payments Interface (UPI) are integral components of the India Stack.
3. The India Stack model is primarily designed for developed economies to streamline digital services.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The India Stack is indeed a collection of open APIs and digital public goods. Statement 2 is correct. Aadhaar (identity layer) and UPI (payments layer) are foundational components. Statement 3 is incorrect. While it can be adopted globally, the India Stack model is particularly showcased as a replicable model for developing economies to achieve digital inclusion and transformation, as highlighted by the Global Digital Public Infrastructure Summit.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a primary focus area of the recently launched 'National Urban Flood Resilience Mission'?
options: (a) Integrated urban planning and upgrading stormwater drainage systems.
(b) Promoting permeable infrastructure to enhance water absorption capacity.
(c) Developing new satellite constellations exclusively for flood monitoring.
(d) Leveraging advanced meteorological forecasting models for early warning systems.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Developing new satellite constellations exclusively for flood monitoring.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The mission focuses on integrated urban planning, upgrading drainage, permeable infrastructure, and leveraging existing advanced forecasting models for early warning systems. While satellite data is crucial for flood monitoring, the mission's primary focus is on ground-level infrastructure, planning, and existing data utilization, not the development of new, exclusive satellite constellations.

prelimsMocks.question:
With respect to the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), consider the following statements:
1. The Treaty was brokered by the United Nations.
2. It primarily divides the waters of six rivers between India and Pakistan.
3. India has exclusive use of the waters of the Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej rivers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The Indus Waters Treaty was brokered by the World Bank, not the United Nations. Statement 2 is incorrect. The treaty divides the waters of six rivers, but allocates the eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India for exclusive use, and the western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan, with India having certain limited rights for non-consumptive uses on the western rivers. So it's not simply 'dividing the waters' of all six equally. Statement 3 is correct. India has exclusive rights to the waters of the Eastern Rivers: Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's recent verdict on the Delhi Services Act has significant implications for the federal structure and governance of Union Territories with legislative assemblies. Critically analyze the judgment's impact on the balance of power between the Union and the NCT of Delhi, and discuss the challenges inherent in administering such unique constitutional arrangements. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure. Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the Delhi Services Act and the SC verdict. Discuss how it reaffirms Parliament's authority, potentially limiting the elected government's power. Analyze the unique constitutional position of Delhi (Article 239AA) and the inherent tensions between federal principles and the special administrative needs of a national capital. Conclude on the implications for cooperative federalism and future governance models for similar entities.

mainsMocks.question: India's ambition to achieve 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030 is crucial for its climate commitments and energy security. Discuss the current progress, key bottlenecks, and the strategic importance of energy storage solutions in realizing this target. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc. Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
mainsMocks.approach: Start by stating India's renewable energy target and its significance. Outline the progress made (e.g., in solar/wind). Detail key bottlenecks like land acquisition, grid integration, and financing. Emphasize the strategic importance of energy storage (BESS, pumped hydro) for grid stability and managing intermittency. Conclude with forward-looking measures to overcome challenges and achieve the target.

mainsMocks.question: The 'India Stack' model, encompassing initiatives like UPI and Aadhaar, is being showcased globally as a successful example of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI). Analyze the transformative potential of DPI for inclusive growth and public service delivery in developing countries, while also discussing the associated challenges of data privacy and cybersecurity. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life. Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology. Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
mainsMocks.approach: Define DPI and introduce the India Stack (UPI, Aadhaar, DigiLocker). Explain its transformative potential for financial inclusion, e-governance, and efficient service delivery in developing countries. Discuss how it helps achieve SDGs. Subsequently, address critical challenges such as ensuring data privacy, building robust cybersecurity frameworks, and bridging the digital divide for equitable access. Conclude on India's role in promoting digital public goods globally.

quickBytes.text: The 69th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1991 inserted Article 239AA into the Indian Constitution, granting special status to Delhi.
India is the world's third-largest energy consuming country and aims for 500 GW renewable energy capacity by 2030.
ISRO's Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) was India's first interplanetary mission, launched in 2013.
Project Tiger was launched in 1973 to conserve tigers in their natural habitats in India.
The GST Council is a constitutional body, chaired by the Union Finance Minister, responsible for making recommendations on GST.
The India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) was announced at the G20 Summit in New Delhi in 2023.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) oversees the implementation of the Indus Waters Treaty between India and Pakistan.
The National Education Policy 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) is responsible for archaeological research and the protection of cultural heritage.
Wootz steel, known for its high carbon content and distinctive patterns, was produced in ancient India and exported globally.

GS1.headline: ASI Report Highlights Growing Threat of Climate Change to India's Ancient Heritage Sites
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) has released a comprehensive report detailing the escalating threats posed by climate change to India's vast array of ancient monuments and heritage sites.
• Extreme weather events, including increased rainfall, floods, droughts, and rising sea levels, are accelerating erosion, structural damage, and the degradation of historical structures.
• The report identifies coastal sites, ancient caves, and monuments built with sensitive materials as particularly vulnerable, requiring urgent adaptive conservation strategies.
• Recommendations include the use of advanced climate modeling, sustainable construction practices for restoration, and the establishment of buffer zones around vulnerable sites.
• This highlights the critical intersection of cultural heritage preservation and environmental sustainability, urging for integrated policy approaches.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Report by Archaeological Survey of India (ASI).
Highlights climate change threat to heritage sites.
Threats include erosion, structural damage from extreme weather.
Coastal sites and caves are highly vulnerable.
Recommends climate modeling and sustainable restoration.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted impacts of climate change on India's tangible cultural heritage and its implications for conservation efforts.
Analyze the challenges faced by organizations like ASI in protecting ancient monuments from climate-induced degradation.
Examine the need for integrated policy frameworks that combine cultural heritage preservation with environmental sustainability.
Suggest innovative and sustainable strategies for the adaptive conservation of vulnerable heritage sites in India.
GS1.imageDescription: Ancient temple ruins showing signs of weathering and erosion.
GS1.trivia: The Ajanta Caves, Elephanta Caves, and the Sun Temple at Konark are among the UNESCO World Heritage Sites managed by the ASI.

• Experts emphasize the need to revive and integrate traditional knowledge systems with modern scientific research for sustainable material development.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Constitutionality of Delhi Services Act, Reinforcing Parliament's Authority
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark verdict, has upheld the constitutional validity of the Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi (Amendment) Act, 2023.
• The ruling reaffirms the Parliament's legislative competence over services in the NCT of Delhi, settling the long-standing dispute between the Union Government and the Delhi elected government.
• The court emphasized the unique status of Delhi as a Union Territory with a legislative assembly, balancing federal principles with the special administrative needs of the national capital.
• This decision is expected to streamline governance in Delhi, providing clarity on the administrative control over bureaucrats and their postings.
• Critics argue the judgment might further curtail the powers of the elected government, while proponents welcome the clarity it brings to Delhi's administrative structure.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary—Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
keyTerms: Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi Act (GNCTD Act)
prelimsPoints: The GNCTD (Amendment) Act, 2023 was challenged in the Supreme Court.
The Act pertains to the control over services (bureaucracy) in the NCT of Delhi.
Article 239AA of the Constitution grants special status to Delhi.
The ruling clarifies the legislative competence of Parliament over Delhi's services.
A Constitutional Bench delivered the judgment.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on the balance of power between the elected government of Delhi and the Union government.
Discuss the challenges of administering a Union Territory with a legislative assembly, citing the Delhi model.
Evaluate the federal principles at play in the context of Article 239AA and the unique status of Delhi.
Examine how such judgments impact cooperative federalism and the autonomy of state/UT governments.
GS2.imageDescription: Gavel and Indian Constitution in the backdrop of the Supreme Court building.
GS2.trivia: Delhi became a Union Territory in 1956 and was given a legislative assembly in 1991 through the 69th Constitutional Amendment Act, which inserted Article 239AA.

GS2.headline: India and Brazil Announce Joint Satellite Mission for Climate Monitoring and Disaster Management
GS2.explainer:
• India (ISRO) and Brazil (AEB) have formalized an agreement for a joint satellite mission aimed at enhancing climate monitoring and disaster management capabilities.
• The mission will involve the development and launch of an advanced earth observation satellite, integrating ISRO's expertise in satellite technology with Brazil's extensive experience in Amazon rainforest and agricultural monitoring.
• This collaboration underscores the growing strategic partnership between the two BRICS nations in critical areas like space technology and environmental sustainability.
• Data from the satellite will be crucial for tracking deforestation, agricultural productivity, water resource management, and providing early warnings for natural disasters.
• The initiative is also a step towards strengthening South-South cooperation in space science and its applications for global challenges.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.

The mission is for climate monitoring and disaster management.
It involves an advanced earth observation satellite.
Brazil is a BRICS member.
Applications include deforestation tracking and early warning systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's growing space diplomacy and its role in fostering South-South cooperation.
Analyze how joint space missions contribute to addressing global challenges like climate change and disaster resilience.
Examine the strategic implications of India-Brazil partnership in advanced technologies for both nations.
Evaluate the potential economic and environmental benefits of such collaborations for developing countries.
GS2.imageDescription: Conceptual image of a satellite orbiting Earth with national flags of India and Brazil.
GS2.trivia: Brazil was the first South American nation to launch its own satellite, SCD-1, in 1993. India has launched satellites for several international partners.

GS2.headline: India-Japan 2+2 Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Maritime Security and Defence Technology Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue involving foreign and defence ministers concluded in Tokyo, focusing on strengthening strategic ties.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, expressing concerns over regional security challenges.
• Key agreements were reached on enhancing maritime security cooperation, including joint exercises and intelligence sharing in the Indian Ocean and Pacific.
• Discussions also covered collaboration in defence technology, co-production, and joint research and development, particularly in advanced materials and AI for defence applications.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between India and Japan in maintaining regional stability and economic prosperity.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: The 2+2 Dialogue involves Foreign and Defence Ministers.
Held in Tokyo.
Focus on Indo-Pacific, maritime security.
Cooperation in defence technology and joint R&D discussed.
India and Japan are members of the Quad.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-Japan 2+2 dialogue in strengthening their 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
Discuss how India and Japan's collaboration contributes to the vision of a free and open Indo-Pacific.
Examine the potential for defence technology cooperation between India and Japan, considering India's 'Make in India' initiative.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in bilateral security cooperation amidst evolving geopolitical dynamics.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan side by side, symbolizing diplomatic relations.
GS2.trivia: The first India-Japan 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue was held in 2019.

GS2.headline: Progress on India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor Discussed at Bilateral Meeting
GS2.explainer:
• High-level officials from India and key Gulf nations held discussions to review the progress and expedite implementation of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC).
• The meeting focused on finalizing investment frameworks, logistical agreements, and regulatory harmonization to ensure seamless connectivity across the corridor.
• IMEC is envisioned as a transformative infrastructure project, aiming to enhance trade, energy flow, and digital connectivity between Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.
• Challenges related to geopolitical stability, funding mechanisms, and coordination among multiple stakeholders were addressed.
• The corridor is seen as a strategic response to global supply chain vulnerabilities and a significant step towards greater economic integration.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.

Aims to enhance trade, energy, and digital connectivity.
Involves multiple nations and stakeholders.
Discussions focused on investment and logistics.
Seen as a response to supply chain vulnerabilities.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) for India.
Discuss the potential geopolitical implications of IMEC in the context of global power dynamics and competing connectivity initiatives.
Examine the challenges in implementing a large-scale multimodal infrastructure project like IMEC involving multiple countries and diverse interests.
Evaluate how IMEC could impact global trade routes, energy security, and regional economic development.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the proposed route of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor.
GS2.trivia: IMEC was announced during the G20 Summit in New Delhi in September 2023.

GS2.headline: Government Proposes Amendments to Lokpal Act to Strengthen Anti-Corruption Framework
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Government has initiated consultations for proposed amendments to the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013, aiming to enhance its operational effectiveness and jurisdiction.
• The amendments seek to address identified lacunae in the existing law, particularly concerning the appointment process, investigative powers, and timely disposal of corruption cases.
• Discussions include broadening the scope of individuals covered under the Lokpal's ambit and ensuring greater autonomy and financial independence for the institution.
• The move comes amidst ongoing efforts to bolster India's anti-corruption framework and promote transparency and accountability in public administration.
• Critics emphasize the need for robust safeguards to prevent misuse of power and ensure due process during investigations.
gsSubTopics: Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Transparency & Accountability and institutional and other measures.
prelimsPoints: Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 established the Lokpal.
Amendments aim to enhance operational effectiveness and jurisdiction.
Focus on appointment process, investigative powers, and case disposal.
Seeks to broaden Lokpal's ambit.
Part of India's anti-corruption efforts.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the Lokpal institution in promoting good governance and combating corruption in India.
Analyze the challenges faced by the Lokpal since its inception and how the proposed amendments seek to address them.
Evaluate the need for a strong, independent anti-corruption ombudsman in a democratic setup.
Examine the balance between empowering anti-corruption bodies and ensuring safeguards against potential misuse of power.
GS2.imageDescription: Logo of Lokpal of India, symbolizing anti-corruption efforts.
GS2.trivia: The concept of an ombudsman originated in Sweden in the early 19th century.

GS2.headline: Review of National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Highlights Progress and Areas for Improvement
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a comprehensive review of the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, three years after its launch.
• Significant progress has been noted in curriculum reforms, integration of vocational education, and the use of technology for learning and administration.
• Challenges persist in achieving universal foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN), faculty recruitment, and infrastructure development, especially in rural areas.
• The review emphasized the need for greater state-level ownership and resource allocation to effectively translate policy vision into ground-level impact.
• NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet the needs of the 21st century, focusing on holistic development and critical thinking.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Examine the role of technology and vocational education in making India's education system more inclusive and employment-oriented.
Suggest strategies for effective state-level implementation and resource mobilization for NEP 2020 objectives.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom, symbolizing education reform.
GS2.trivia: The NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education, which was modified in 1992.

GS2.headline: Permanent Indus Commission Meeting Underway to Address Water-Sharing Issues
GS2.explainer:
• The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC), comprising water commissioners from India and Pakistan, has commenced its annual meeting to discuss various aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT).
• The agenda includes technical discussions on flood data sharing, usage of waters of the western rivers, and resolution of minor disputes related to hydroelectric projects.
• The meeting aims to ensure continued cooperation and adherence to the provisions of the IWT, a crucial water-sharing agreement between the two nations.
• While the treaty has largely withstood geopolitical tensions, concerns over project designs and hydrological data exchange often surface during these discussions.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank, is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties globally, despite intermittent bilateral strains.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) meets annually.
Discusses Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) between India and Pakistan.
IWT was brokered by the World Bank.
Eastern rivers for India, Western rivers for Pakistan (with some Indian usage rights).
Agenda includes flood data and hydroelectric projects.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance and enduring success of the Indus Waters Treaty amidst India-Pakistan geopolitical tensions.
Analyze the role of the Permanent Indus Commission in resolving water-sharing disputes and ensuring treaty compliance.
Examine the challenges and potential areas of conflict related to the IWT in the context of climate change and increasing water demand.
Evaluate the importance of water diplomacy in maintaining regional stability in South Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River System and the distribution of rivers under the IWT.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty was signed in Karachi on September 19, 1960, by Indian Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru and Pakistani President Ayub Khan.

GS2.headline: India-Sri Lanka Fisheries Dialogue Resumes to Resolve Long-Standing Disputes
GS2.explainer:
• A fresh round of bilateral talks between India and Sri Lanka has commenced to address the contentious issue of fishing rights and persistent disputes in the Palk Strait.
• The dialogue aims to find a permanent solution to the problem of Indian fishermen allegedly poaching in Sri Lankan waters, leading to arrests and seizure of boats.
• Discussions include exploring alternative livelihood options for Indian fishermen, joint patrolling mechanisms, and sustainable fishing practices.
• Both sides acknowledge the humanitarian aspect of the issue while emphasizing the need to protect marine resources and the livelihoods of their respective fishing communities.
• Resolving this issue is crucial for maintaining cordial bilateral relations and regional maritime stability.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Talks between India and Sri Lanka on fishing rights.
Disputes occur in the Palk Strait.
Issue of Indian fishermen allegedly poaching in Sri Lankan waters.
Katchatheevu Island is a relevant geographical point.
Focus on sustainable fishing and alternative livelihoods.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and complexities of the India-Sri Lanka fisheries dispute in the Palk Strait.
Analyze the socio-economic implications of the dispute for fishing communities on both sides.
Evaluate the effectiveness of various diplomatic and policy measures taken to resolve this long-standing issue.
Suggest sustainable solutions that can balance the livelihoods of fishermen with marine ecological conservation.
GS2.imageDescription: Fishing boats in the Palk Strait, symbolizing the disputed waters.
GS2.trivia: Katchatheevu, an uninhabited island in the Palk Strait, was ceded to Sri Lanka by India in 1974.

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Telemedicine Services to Remote Rural Areas
GS2.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) has announced a significant expansion of its telemedicine services, specifically targeting remote and underserved rural areas.
• This initiative aims to bridge the healthcare access gap by leveraging digital platforms to connect patients with doctors and specialists across the country.
• The expansion includes setting up more digital health kiosks, training local health workers in digital literacy, and ensuring last-mile internet connectivity.
• It seeks to reduce the burden on tertiary care hospitals and provide timely medical consultations, especially for non-critical conditions and follow-ups.
• The move aligns with the broader goal of universal health coverage and equitable access to quality healthcare services.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
